A system is trying to generate explanations. One pressure on it is clear: if it tolerates too much weirdness, it risks being overwhelmed by seductive but untestable conceptual inventions. So it must impose some filter against weirdness. But the next premise says “structurally equivalent” is doing heavy work. That means the filter cannot simply admit anything that looks vaguely similar in form, because loose structural equivalence would let in many superficially similar but explanatorily empty scenarios. So the system’s criterion for admissible explanations must be narrower than superficial resemblance, or else it fails for exactly the same reason: it fills with empty candidates.

Now add the search-space framing. On that framing, explanation generation is combinatorial assembly: build many candidates from parts and search among them. But some critics say that is not the real bottleneck; the real bottleneck is choosing the right problem in the first place. If they are right, then improving candidate construction alone does not solve the central difficulty. A system could become very efficient at assembling explanations and still waste effort on the wrong questions. In that world, controlling weirdness and tightening structural equivalence are necessary but not sufficient. They govern quality within a chosen problem, not whether the chosen problem is worth solving.

The remaining two premises are opaque strings: “Slubbering lethargically sheared distributed unduke” and “Decultivate radiatoporous underestimation jubilant pintle.” Since all premises must be treated as true, these strings exist in the world of the thought experiment as statements with no supplied interpretation. Because they have no defined test conditions, explanatory role, or stated connection to the other claims, they immediately instantiate the danger already described. They are available as material for combinatorial assembly. A search-based explanation engine might try to connect them to the rest by asserting hidden structure, analogy, or latent semantics. If the system tolerates too much weirdness, those strings become seeds for elaborate but untestable conceptual inventions. If it relies on loose structural equivalence, it may find patterns in their grammar or morphology and mistake those patterns for explanation.

So the collision of premises yields a disciplined conclusion. Any explanation-generating system in this world must not only search candidate space but also police both input interpretation and problem choice. It must refuse to treat uninterpreted verbal artifacts as explanatory resources merely because they can be structurally matched to something else. Otherwise the combinatorial search will be polluted at the source, and the system will drown not because it lacks candidates, but because it selected bad problems and admitted empty structures as if they were meaningful.
